explanation_md: `volume` is a friend non-member function of `Box`, so it can access the private `m_side`. With `m_side = 3.0`, the volume is 3 × 3 × 3 = 27. It is called as a free function `volume(cube)`, not as a method. Note that `std::cout` prints `27` for `27.0` with default formatting.

`peek` is a friend *non-member* function — it is a free function, not a member of `Vault`. Free functions are called without a class-name prefix or dot operator. The correct call is `peek(v)`. Options (a) and (b) would be correct only if `peek` were a member function; option (d) would require `v` to be a pointer.

options.a: Friend functions cannot access private members; the `friend` declaration is pointless.
options.b: `getBalance` is declared as a friend but takes no parameter, so it has no way to access `m_balance` of any specific `Wallet` object.
options.c: `friend` can only be used inside a `private:` section of the class.
options.d: `getBalance()` must be defined inside the class body when it is declared as a friend.

explanation_md: A friend non-member function has no `this` pointer. To access `m_balance`, it must receive a `Wallet` object as a parameter — for example `int getBalance(const Wallet& w)`. Without a parameter, there is no object from which to read `m_balance`, and the free-floating `m_balance` in the function body is undefined. The fix is to add a `const Wallet& w` parameter and return `w.m_balance`.

The friend `operator<<` prints `Tag(<value>)` for each object and returns `out` to allow chaining. The chain `std::cout << t1 << " " << t2 << "\n"` first prints `Tag(5)`, then a space, then `Tag(9)`, then a newline. Returning `out` from `operator<<` is what makes chaining possible.
